# Content Exclusion Patterns

This file is a **reference copy** of the glob patterns to paste into GitHub's Copilot Content Exclusion UI (*Settings* -> *Copilot* -> *Content Exclusion*, at repository or organization level).

Content Exclusion itself is not configured by a committed file — these patterns are kept here so the exclusion set is version-controlled and reviewable, not because Copilot reads this file directly.

## Patterns

```
build/**
dist/**
generated/**
node_modules/**
package-lock.json
yarn.lock
__pycache__/**
*.pyc
.pytest_cache/**
.venv/**
.vscode/**
.idea/**
.DS_Store
```

## Why Each Pattern

| Pattern              | Reason                                                                       |
| -------------------- | ---------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| `build/`, `dist/`, `generated/` | Compiled/generated output — never edit source, always out of date       |
| `node_modules/`      | Vendored dependencies — huge surface area, dilutes suggestion relevance      |
| `package-lock.json`, `yarn.lock` | Autogenerated; editing manually is almost always wrong                   |
| `__pycache__/`, `*.pyc`, `.pytest_cache/`, `.venv/` | Python runtime/test caches; not source                |
| `.vscode/`, `.idea/`, `.DS_Store` | Editor and OS noise; not project content                              |

## Where to Apply

- **Repository level** — scope this pattern list to a single repository when the patterns are project-specific.
- **Organization level** — lift this list to the org when the same pattern set applies across many repositories (most orgs do).
- **User level** — Content Exclusion does not have a per-user surface; individuals who cannot set repo/org exclusions can still reduce exposure by avoiding opening the noise-heavy files while using Copilot.

## What Content Exclusion Does *Not* Do

- It does not hide the files from your editor. VS Code still shows them; you can still open them.
- It does not prevent the agent from reading a file if it explicitly invokes a filesystem tool with an absolute path. Use MCP tool permissions and `preToolUse` hooks for that (see the MCP Tool Security lab).
- It does not replace `.gitignore`. The two have different purposes: `.gitignore` controls Git's index, Content Exclusion controls Copilot's context.
